text:
Tony Blair will be remembered for many things as the Prime Minister who wanted to be President and lost his smile in Chicago when his idol, George W. Bush, refused him a real role at the world leader’s podium in the second invasion of Iraq. But Revd. A.R.P. Blair, T Blair “ vicar of the people” preaching from the pulpit of St. Albion’s cathedral, though something of a private eye caricature, still rang a bell, because very few people (apart from Blair himself and his family) really believe he can divide the Rev and the PM out of existence as his special advisers famously put it, when they replaced his prayer breakfasts and Bible readings with a motto that ran, “we don’t do God” Even as leader of the opposition this Christian salesman in designer suit robes was very keen to give people glimpses of the genuinely religious man, who after the Lord had brought him safely to the promised land made almost an effort to keep him below the water line.

There is no doubt that religion has affected Tony Blair in a profound way and almost certainly affects Gordon Brown in similar ways though this aspect of the Prime Minister's faith has not so far been publicly highlighted in any significant detail. Yet as yet the politics of God has been treated with scant regard by political scientists and analysts who have preferred to “bracket out” religion as a factor of relevance to political study. No more than that is required in order to validate the assertion that the unwillingness to ‘do God’ in the world of political communication is matched in equal and opposite fashion by the reluctance of those in the world of political science to genuinely explore the factor of religion in politics.

Faith and New Labour: What’s public about ‘public faith’? Anyone who seeks to discuss the role of faith in New Labour’s project has to discuss it in terms of public discourse, rather than private disposition. The remoralisation of Britain-from ethical foreign policy and social inclusion to the ‘war on terror’ has been associated with a vocabulary taken directly from the tradition of Christian socialism. That religion has re-entered the public domain is thus directly at odds with a secularisation of everyday life that has taken place in a way that is both constitutionally embedded – in a legal framework which continues to affirm the principle of laicity – and culturally sedimented – at a moment when the British is at an all-time low and in which the secularisation thesis is an unquestionable hegemonic ‘common sense’.

Secular Space/Belonging Publics: The politics of ‘tension’ This chapter describes the shifting borders between private and public space in liberal democracies, to question the assumption of a space free of values in politics. Emerging understandings of procedural secularism and religious literacy which recognize the religious pluralism of public spaces will be examined. Finally, a focus on the ‘soul’ of New Labour politics – recognizable in tones both comic and otherworldly – will be required as a ruse to look beneath its rather mesmerizing surface to the workings of religion in politics in this post-secular age.
